titleStmt.title: SII 3.15: original edition by Eugen Julius Theodor Hultzsch
No. 15.‚ÄĒON THE BASE OF THE CHOLESVARA SHRINE.

summary.p: This inscription is dated in the 29th year of the reign of the ChŇćŠł∑a king RńĀjarńĀja I. and opens with the usual historical introduction, where, however, this inscription and No. 16 read TaŠłćńęga-pńĀŠłći instead of TaŠłćigai-pńĀŠłći or TaŠłćiya-vaŠłĽi.See above, page 5.

The inscription records that the citizens of MńďŠĻüpńĀŠłći granted to the AŠĻüi√Ījigai-ń™Ňõvara temple 5, 136(1/2) kuŠłĽi of land, which was bounded in the east by the river NugńĀ, and in the north by the ChŇćŠł∑ńďndrasiŠĻĀhńďŇõvara temple. NugńĀ is evidently the original name of the river NńęvńĀ (or PoŠĻČŠĻČai), on the western bank of which MńďlpńĀŠłći is situated, and ChŇćŠł∑ńďndrasiŠĻĀhńďŇõvara is the ancient designation of the SŇćmanńĀthńďŇõvara temple.See the preceding page, and below, p. 29.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
svasti Ňõrńę . tirumakaŠł∑ polapperunilaccelviyun=taŠĻČakkeyurimai pŇęŠĻáŠĻ≠amai maŠĻČakkoŠł∑kkńĀn=taŠł∑urRead Šł∑Ňęr. cńĀlai kalamaŠĻüuttaruŠł∑i veŠĻÖkainńĀŠĻ≠uŠĻÖkaŠĻÖkapńĀŠĻ≠iyunuŠł∑ampapńĀŠĻ≠iyuntaŠĻ≠ńękapńĀŠĻ≠iyuŠĻÖkuŠĻ≠amalainńĀŠĻ≠uŠĻÖkollamuŠĻÖkaliŠĻÖkamumeŠĻáŠĻ≠icai pukaŠłĽ taravńęŠłĽamaŠĻáŠĻ≠alamumiraŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠apńĀŠĻ≠iyeŠłĽaraiyilakkamun=tiŠĻáŠĻ≠iŠĻüal veŠĻČŠĻüittaŠĻáŠĻ≠ńĀŠĻükkoŠĻáŠĻ≠a tanŠĻČeŠłĽil vaŠł∑arŇęŠłĽi EllńĀyńĀŠĻáŠĻ≠un=toŠłĽutakai viŠł∑aŠĻÖkum yńĀŠĻáŠĻ≠ey ceŠłĽiyaraittecu koŠł∑ ŇõrńękovirńĀjarńĀjakesaripanmarńĀna ŇõrńęrńĀjarńĀjadevarkku yńĀŠĻáŠĻ≠u 2 10 9 ńÄvatu jayaŠĻÖkoŠĻáŠĻ≠acoŠłĽamaŠĻáŠĻ≠alattupperumpńĀŠĻáappńĀŠĻ≠ittŇę√ĪńĀŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠u meŠĻüppńĀŠĻ≠iyńĀkiya rńĀjńĀŇõrayapurattu nakarattomRead ńĀŠĻüŠĻüŇęr. ńÄŠĻüŠĻüurarttu√ĪciŠĻČa tevarkkuppaŠł∑Šł∑ipaŠĻ≠aiyńĀka UŠĻ≠aiyńĀr ŇõrńęrńĀjarńĀjadevar EŠĻÖkaŠł∑ nakarattileŠĻ≠uppittaruŠł∑iŠĻČa tiru AŠĻüi√ĪcikaińęŇõvarattu mahńĀdevar [Ňõrńę]koyilukkuntiruccuŠĻüŠĻüńĀlaikkuntirumuŠĻüŠĻüattukkuntirunantaŠĻČavaŠĻČattukkum
maŠĻ≠aviŠł∑ńĀkattukkumńĀka nńĀŠĻÖkaŠł∑ittevaŠĻükukkuŠĻ≠utta nilattukkellai nukńĀveŠĻČŠĻČumńĀŠĻüŠĻüukku meŠĻükum IvvŇęr mummaŠĻ≠icoŠłĽapperunteruvil vyńĀpńĀri ńÄccerumńĀŠĻČ vayiramekaŠĻČ toŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠appńĀŠłĽńĀŠĻČa nilattiŠĻČ vaŠĻ≠avarukey nukńĀvńĀŠĻüŠĻüiniŠĻČŠĻüumirukolakalattńĀl meŠĻüku nokkippoŠĻČa peruvaŠłĽi EŠĻápatteŠłĽukolńĀcaŠĻüutikku vaŠĻ≠akkumivvńĀcaŠĻüutiyey vaŠĻ≠akku nokkiccoŠł∑entrasiŠĻĀ[hi]- ŇõvaramuŠĻ≠aiyaRead siŠĻĀhńęŇõvara. tevar tevaraŠĻ≠iyńĀr mattavńĀŠĻáacceriyeyeŠĻüappoŠĻČa teruvukkukkiŠłĽakku√Īco [Šł∑entra] siŠĻĀhńęŇõvaramuŠĻ≠aiyńĀr koyiliŠĻČ teŠĻükil tirunantaŠĻČavaŠĻČattiŠĻČ teŠĻČŠĻČarukey nukaraveŠĻČŠĻČumńĀŠĻüŠĻüukkey kiŠłĽakku nokki nńĀŠĻükolakalattńĀŠĻüpoŠĻČa peruvaŠłĽikkutteŠĻükuminnńĀŠĻüperellaiyuŠł∑Šł∑umakappaŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠a nilamRead eŠĻÖkaŠł∑Ňęra. eŠĻÖkaŠł∑uraŠł∑antu kŇęŠĻüiŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠a patiŠĻČeŠĻácńĀŠĻáko(l)lńĀŠĻükuŠłĽi AiyyńĀyirattorunuraŠĻüŠĻüuRead nŇęŠĻüŠĻüu. muppattńĀŠĻüaraiyumittevaŠĻükuttevatńĀŠĻČa IŠĻüaiyiliyńĀkakkuŠĻ≠uttom . InnilattńĀŠĻüey nukaraveŠĻČŠĻČumńĀŠĻüŠĻüiŠĻČiŠĻČŠĻüum Itte
varinnilattukkumiluppaikkaŠłĽaŠĻČikkunńęr pńĀynta vńĀykkńĀl kńĀŠĻükolakalattńĀl IttevarinnilattukkumiluppaikkaŠłĽaŠĻČikkunirRead nńęr. pńĀyappeŠĻüavumipparicu tevatńĀŠĻČaiŠĻüaiyiliyńĀkaccilńĀlekai ceytu kuŠĻ≠uttomeŠĻüpńĀŠĻ≠iyńĀŠĻČa rńĀjńĀŇõrayapurattu nakarattom . InnakarattńĀr colla EŠłĽutiŠĻČeŠĻČ InnakarakkaraŠĻáattńĀŠĻČ nńĀrńĀyaŠĻáaŠĻČaŠĻ≠aikkalavaŠĻČeŠĻČ . IvaiyeŠĻČŠĻČeŠłĽattuRead ŠłĽuttu. ..itu paŠĻČmńĀbheŇõvararakŠĻ£aiRead mńĀhńďŇõvara. ..

(Line 1.) Hail ! Prosperity ! In the 29th year (of the reign) of the glorious king RńĀjarńĀja-Kńďsarivarman, alias Ňörńę-RńĀjarńĀjadńďva, who, (in) his life of growing strength, during which,‚ÄĒ(in) the belief that, as well as the goddess of fortune, the goddess of the great earth had become his wife,‚ÄĒ(he) was pleased to destroy the ships (at) KńĀndaŠł∑Ňęr-ŇöńĀlai and conquered by (his) army, which was victorious in great battles, VńďŠĻÖgai-nńĀŠłću, GaŠĻÖga-pńĀŠłći, NuŠł∑amba-pńĀŠłći, TaŠłćńęga-pńĀŠłći, KuŠłćamalai-nńĀŠłću, Kollam, KaliŠĻÖgam, ń™ŠłĽa-maŠĻáŠłćalam, (the conquest of which) made (him) famous (in) the eight directions, and the seven and a half lakshas of IraŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠a-pńĀŠłći,‚ÄĒdeprived the ŇöeŠłĽiyas of (their) splendour at the very moment when Udag[ai], which is worshipped everywhere, was (most) resplendent;‚ÄĒwe, the citizens of MńďŠĻüpńĀŠłći, alias RńĀjńĀŇõrayapuram, in TŇę√ĪńĀŠłću, (a subdivision) of PerumbńĀŠĻáa-pńĀŠłći in JayaŠĻÖkoŠĻáŠłća-ŇöŇćŠłĽa-maŠĻáŠłćalam, gave land to the god MahńĀdńďva (Ňöiva) of the holy AŠĻüi√Ījigai-ń™Ňõvara (temple),‚ÄĒwhich the lord Ňörńę-RńĀjarńĀjadńďva had been pleased to build in our city as a resting-place (? paŠł∑Šł∑i-paŠłćai) for the king who fell asleepIn the Appendix to PaŠĻáŠłćit ŇöńĀminńĀdaiyar's edition of the PuŠĻüanńĀŠĻČŇęŠĻüu, Mr. V.Kanakasabhai Pillai suggests that the expression tu√ĪjiŠĻČa or tu√Ījiya, ‚Äėwho slept,‚Äô which forms part of the designations of several ChŇćŠł∑a, PńĀŠĻáŠłćya and Chńďra kings, means ‚Äėwho died.‚Äô at ńÄŠĻüŠĻüŇęr,‚ÄĒfor the sacred temple, for the temple enclosure,ńĀlai is a corruption of ńĀlaiyam (ńĀlaya in Sanskrit). Hence tiruccuŠĻüŠĻüńĀlai is synonymous with tiruc cuŠĻüŠĻüumńĀŠł∑ikai and parivńĀrńĀlaya, on which see Vol. II. p. 151, note 1. for the temple court, for the temple garden, and for the environs of the temple.Compare Vol. I. p. 92, note 5.

(L. 2.) The (four) boundaries of (this land are) to the west of the river called NugńĀ; to the north of the limit, eighty-seven rods (in length), of the high-road which, at a breadth of two rods, leads westward from the NugńĀ river along the northern extremity of the waste land of the garden of the merchant (vyńĀpńĀrin) ńÄchcherumńĀŠĻČ VayiramńďgaŠĻČ, (who resides) in the high-street of MummaŠłći-ŇöŇćŠłĽa in this village; to the east of the street which leads up northward from this limit to MattavńĀŠĻáachchńďri, (the quarter) of the dancing-girls of the god of the ŇöŇćŠł∑ńďndrasiŠĻĀhńęŇõvara (temple); and to the south of the high-road which, at a breadth of four rods, leads eastward to the river called NugńĀ along the southern extremity of the temple garden on the south of the temple of the lord ŇöŇćŠł∑ńďndrasiŠĻĀhńęŇõvara. The land enclosed within these four great boundaries, (which measures) five thousand one hundred and thirty-six and a half kuŠłĽi by the rod of eighteen spans, by which our village is measured and divided, we gave to this god as tax-free temple land. The channel, a quarter rod in breadth, (which flows) through this land from the river called NugńĀ (and) supplies water to this land and to the iluppaiThis is the Tamil name of the tree Bassia longifolia. field of this god, shall (continue to) supply water to this land and to the iluppai field of this god.

(L. 3.) Thus we, the citizens of MńďŠĻüpńĀŠłći, alias RńĀjńĀŇõrayapuram, having engraved (this) on stone, gave (this land) as tax-free temple land. At the bidding of these citizens, I, the accountant (karaŠĻáattńĀŠĻČ) of this city, NńĀrńĀyaŠĻáaŠĻČ AŠłćaikkalavaŠĻČ, wrote (this). This is my writing. This (gift is placed under) the protection of all MńĀhńďŇõvaras.
Digital edition of SII 3.15 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
